profile.behavior: Ground-foraging thrush that hops and scrapes through leaf litter; often flicks its wings and slowly bobs or raises its tail.
field_marks: Warm reddish-brown tail contrasting with the browner back
Thin pale eye-ring around a dark eye
Pale underparts with dark spots on the throat and smudgy breast spotting
Round-headed, full-bodied profile with a slender straight bill and fairly long tail
profile.habitat: Forest understory and floor, especially in deciduous, coniferous, or mixed woodland with edges, openings, shrubs, and leaf litter.

sources.title: Hermit Thrush Identification — All About Birds

findings: Verified identity: the plate depicts a Hermit Thrush (Catharus guttatus) with the diagnostic brown upperparts, contrasting warm rufous tail, thin pale eye-ring, pale spotted throat/breast, round head, slender straight bill, and fairly long tail. These match the reference photos and the Cornell and Audubon guides.

All visible plate text is factually supported. The stated 14–18 cm length, 25–29 cm wingspan, and 23–37 g weight match Cornell exactly; Audubon gives a slightly different published length/wingspan range (16–19 cm; 28–30 cm), so the plate's figures remain valid rather than erroneous.

The bird has one head, one beak, two legs, and one tail. Its two wings are anatomically coherent: one is visible on the perched bird and the detached wing is clearly a study inset, not an additional attached limb. Feet and toes are somewhat stylized and visually busy, but retain plausible passerine perching anatomy.
No place name, ZIP code, coordinates, map, or local-observation detail appears on the candidate plate.

verification_sources.title: Hermit Thrush Identification — All About Birds
